summary: High‑energy Spectrum show with soaring She Belongs To Me & tight jams
blurb: The set bursts from a punchy Shakedown Street into jam‑heavy Truckin' and climaxes with an emotional Morning Dew > Sugar Magnolia. Jerry’s vocal is rough but his guitar shines on She Belongs To Me, while Phil fuels the momentum with classic crowd‑pleasing intros. Brent’s keyboards add fiery texture throughout.
review: April 7 1985 at The Spectrum captured one of the spring ’85 high‑octane outings that Deadheads still talk about. From the opening “Why Don't We Do It In The Road” through a rollicking first set, the band kept the groove tight and the jokes flowing. The second set is where the night truly ignites: a blistering “Shakedown Street” launches into a soulful “Samson & Delilah,” then Jerry delivers an unforgettable solo on “She Belongs To Me” despite his hoarse voice. Brent’s B‑13 organ swells over “Gimme Some Lovin’,” and the rhythm section—Mickey’s perfectly balanced kick and Billy’s steady pulse—drives a seamless transition into “Truckin' > Smokestack Lightning.” The night ends on an emotional high with “Morning Dew” bleeding straight into a jubilant “Sugar Magnolia,” leaving the crowd buzzing. Across all available sources, the Keith‑sourced soundboard (gd85-04-07.sbd.keith.14614.sbeok.shnf) stands out for its crystal‑clear mix and subtle audience ambience that makes it feel almost matrix‑like, delivering the most faithful representation of this legendary performance.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Strong positive sentiment across the majority of recordings (4 positives, 2 mixed, 1 negative). Reviewers consistently praise the high‑energy set flow, standout songs like Shakedown Street and She Belongs To Me, and Brent's keyboard work, outweighing occasional critiques about vocal roughness or isolated weak moments.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard capture with crisp, well‑balanced mix and subtle audience ambience; widely praised as the clearest representation of the show.

key_highlights: High‑energy Shakedown Street that launches the second set.
Emotional Morning Dew > Sugar Magnolia finale.
Jerry’s soaring solo on She Belongs To Me despite a rough vocal.
Brent Mydland’s fiery B‑13 organ on Gimme Some Lovin’ and tight drum work.

band_performance.Jerry: Vocals are hoarse and shot, but his guitar work shines—especially the solo on She Belongs To Me and the lead on Samson.
band_performance.Phil: Keeps the momentum alive with classic crowd‑pleasing intros and even steps up for a vocal lift on the Half‑Step segment.
band_performance.Brent: Delivers heavy B‑13 organ textures on Gimme Some Lovin' and adds high‑pitched, energetic keyboard squeals throughout the set.
band_performance.Mickey: Kick drum balance is praised as perfect; provides a solid, tight rhythmic foundation alongside Billy.

must_listen_sequences: Why Don't We Do It In The Road
